title: Valid Sudoku Board Backtracking with Duplicate Cell Values

statement.markdown: Given a partially filled-in Sudoku board, determine if it can be completed to form a valid solution. Note that the same cell can be represented multiple times in the input, e.g., both [0, 0] and [0, 1] may represent the same cell in the Sudoku grid.
statement.inputFormat: A 2D array representing a Sudoku board, where each cell is represented by its row and column indices, and the cell's value is the number to be placed in the cell, or 0 for empty cells.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the Sudoku board can be completed to form a valid solution.
statement.notes: The board is represented as a 9x9 2D array, with the same rules for Sudoku board validity applying.

examples.explanation: An empty Sudoku board can be completed to form a valid solution.
An empty Sudoku board can be completed to form a valid solution.

hints.text: Use backtracking to try possible values for each cell.

editorial.title: Valid Sudoku Board Backtracking with Duplicate Cell Values
editorial.intuition: The idea is to try each possible value for each cell and use backtracking to undo the changes when a dead end is reached.
editorial.bruteForce: We can simply try each possible value for each cell without using backtracking.
editorial.betterSolution: Using backtracking can reduce the number of attempts and improve efficiency.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use a set to keep track of the values used in each row, column, and 3x3 sub-grid.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The solution is correct because it correctly identifies whether a Sudoku board can be completed to form a valid solution.
editorial.dryRun: Try the solution on an example board to see how it works.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to update the used set when a cell value is changed.
Not checking for duplicate values in the same cell.
